perturbation.original_text: 1. AMENDMENT OF DEFINITIONS. Article 1 of the Distributor Agreement is hereby amended to add thereto the following: 1.11 "Annual Royalty Advance Requirement" shall mean (i) $1,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 1999, (ii) $2,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2000, (iii) $3,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2001, (iv) $4,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2002 and (v) $5,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2003. In the event that the term hereof extends beyond Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2003, the amount of the Annual Royalty Advance shall increase by $1,000,000 for each such fiscal year thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. AMENDMENT OF DEFINITIONS. Article 1 of the Distributor Agreement is hereby amended to add thereto the following: 1.11 "Annual Royalty Advance Requirement" shall mean (i) $1,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 1999, (ii) $2,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2000, (iii) $3,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2001, (iv) $4,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2002 and (v) $5,000,000 in respect of Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2003. In the event that the term hereof extends beyond Licensee's fiscal year beginning April 1, 2003, the amount of the Annual Royalty Advance shall increase by $1,000,000 for each such fiscal year thereafter. However, the 'Annual Royalty Advance Requirement' for any year after 2003 shall not exceed $6,000,000 regardless of fiscal year extensions.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by capping the 'Annual Royalty Advance Requirement' at $6,000,000 for any year after 2003, despite the previous sentence stating it increases by $1,000,000 per fiscal year. This contradicts the automatic annual increase provision, creating ambiguity in calculating the royalty advances for extended terms. The contradiction specifically arises when considering the implications on Section 3.2, where the Royalty Advances are calculated based on the Annual Royalty Advance Requirement. This contradiction creates uncertainty in the royalty payment schedule.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 3.2 TERMS OF PAYMENT. The royalties payable to Licensor pursuant to Section 3.1 shall be payable in accordance with the provisions of this Section 3.2. On or before the first day of each fiscal quarter of each fiscal year during the term hereof, commencing with the Licensee's fiscal year which begins on April 1, 1999, Licensee shall pay to Licensor, as an advance (a "Royalty Advance") of royalties anticipated to be paid hereunder during such fiscal year, an amount equal to twenty-five percent (25%) of the Annual Royalty Advance Requirement for such fiscal year. The aggregate amount of Royalty Advances outstanding from time to time shall be credited against royalties payable hereunder pursuant to Section 3.1 as and when such royalties are recognized as earned in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles. Royalty Advances made hereunder shall be made in respect of royalties that may become payable in respect of any and all Licensed Products and shall not be deemed made in respect 2 of any particular Licensed Product. With respect to any royalty payments due hereunder from Licensee to Licensor in excess of the amount of Royalty Advances made by Licensee from time to time hereunder, such payments shall be payable on the later of (i) sixty (60) days after the date of the applicable invoice to a Redistributor or Customer, as the case may be, or (ii) five (5) business days following Licensee's receipt of payment from a Redistributor or Customer, as the case may be. Any royalty payment that is not paid when due will bear interest from the date such amount is due until the date payment is made at a rate equal to ten percent (10%) per annum. All royalty payments due to Licensor under this Agreement shall be paid in U.S. Dollars. Upon the expiration or any termination of this Agreement, Licensor shall repay to Licensee the aggregate amount of all Royalty Advances then outstanding.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction by capping the Annual Royalty Advance Requirement at $6,000,000 after 2003, overriding the original provision that mandated an annual increase of $1,000,000 beyond that year. This conflict directly impacts Section 3.2, which calculates royalty advances based on the Annual Royalty Advance Requirement, creating ambiguity and potential disputes regarding the payment schedule, thus meeting the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The provisions of Section 14.2 are amended by deleting in its entirety subsection (b) thereof and by amending Section 14.2(a)(2) to read in its entirety as follows: (2) Immediately upon written notice if the other party defaults in the performance of any obligation under this Agreement, including failure to promptly pay any amount due hereunder, and fails to cure such default within thirty (30) days after delivery of written notice specifying the default (with any termination as a result of Licensee's failure to pay amounts due under this Agreement resulting in acceleration of Licensee's obligation to pay all sums accrued and payable to Licensor under this Agreement as of the date of such termination).
perturbation.changed_text: The provisions of Section 14.2 are amended by deleting in its entirety subsection (b) thereof and by amending Section 14.2(a)(2) to read in its entirety as follows: (2) Immediately upon written notice if the other party defaults in the performance of any obligation under this Agreement, including failure to promptly pay any amount due hereunder, and fails to cure such default within thirty (30) days after delivery of written notice specifying the default (with any termination as a result of Licensee's failure to pay amounts due under this Agreement resulting in acceleration of Licensee's obligation to pay all sums accrued and payable to Licensor under this Agreement as of the date of such termination). However, failure to pay due to documented technical issues related to payment processing systems shall not be considered a default.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the clause 'However, failure to pay due to documented technical issues related to payment processing systems shall not be considered a default,' we introduce ambiguity regarding termination. Section 14.2(a)(2) originally stated that failure to promptly pay is grounds for immediate termination with written notice, resulting in acceleration of all payments. The added clause provides an exception for technical payment issues, making it unclear whether termination and acceleration apply in such cases. This contradicts the original clear consequence of failing to pay, creating uncertainty about when termination and acceleration can be invoked. This contradiction relates to the main termination clause in Section 14.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. AMENDMENT OF TERMINATION PROVISIONS. The provisions of Section 14.2 are amended by deleting in its entirety subsection (b) thereof and by amending Section 14.2(a)(2) to read in its entirety as follows: (2) Immediately upon written notice if the other party defaults in the performance of any obligation under this Agreement, including failure to promptly pay any amount due hereunder, and fails to cure such default within thirty (30) days after delivery of written notice specifying the default (with any termination as a result of Licensee's failure to pay amounts due under this Agreement resulting in acceleration of Licensee's obligation to pay all sums accrued and payable to Licensor under this Agreement as of the date of such termination). However, failure to pay due to documented technical issues related to payment processing systems shall not be considered a default.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the termination provision in Section 14.2(a)(2) regarding failure to pay due to technical issues. This contradicts the original clause, which states that any failure to promptly pay results in immediate termination and acceleration of payments. The addition of 'However, failure to pay due to documented technical issues related to payment processing systems shall not be considered a default' creates uncertainty and a direct conflict, as it's now unclear whether termination and acceleration apply in cases of technical payment issues. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' where key obligations are defined inconsistently.

perturbation.original_text: Section 15.12 OPTION TO PURCHASE. Skunkware and Licensor hereby grant to Licensee the exclusive and irrevocable right and option to purchase (the "Option"), at Licensee's election, either (i) all of the assets of Licensor or (ii) all of the issued and outstanding stock of Licensor. Such option shall be exercisable during a period (the "Option Period") commencing on and including the earlier of (i) the date upon which Licensee shall have paid to Licensor, in any single fiscal year of Licensee, royalty payments hereunder in the aggregate amount of $10,000,000 or (ii) January 1, 2002, and ending upon the expiration or sooner termination of this Agreement. Licensee's exercise of the Option is at its sole discretion. Licensee may exercise the Option by written notice to Licensor and Skunkware at any time during the Option Period. Upon any such exercise of the Option, Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware (as the case may be) shall proceed to diligently and in good faith negotiate and execute a definitive purchase and sale agreement for Licensor's acquisition of all of the assets of, or outstanding capital stock in, Licensor, as the case may be. In the event that Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware, as the case may be, are unable to agree on any terms or conditions for such acquisition, the same shall be submitted to arbitration in accordance with the rules and procedures of the American Arbitration Association, with the arbitrator(s) to be experienced in the mainframe software industry.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 15.12 OPTION TO PURCHASE. Skunkware and Licensor hereby grant to Licensee the exclusive and irrevocable right and option to purchase (the "Option"), at Licensee's election, either (i) all of the assets of Licensor or (ii) all of the issued and outstanding stock of Licensor. Such option shall be exercisable during a period (the "Option Period") commencing on and including the earlier of (i) the date upon which Licensee shall have paid to Licensor, in any single fiscal year of Licensee, royalty payments hereunder in the aggregate amount of $10,000,000 or (ii) January 1, 2002, and ending upon the expiration or sooner termination of this Agreement. Licensee's exercise of the Option is at its sole discretion. Licensee may exercise the Option by written notice to Licensor and Skunkware at any time during the Option Period. Upon any such exercise of the Option, Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware (as the case may be) shall proceed to diligently negotiate and execute a definitive purchase and sale agreement for Licensor's acquisition of all of the assets of, or outstanding capital stock in, Licensor, as the case may be. If Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware, as the case may be, are unable to agree on any terms or conditions for such acquisition, the same shall be submitted to arbitration; however, any decision reached via arbitration shall be non-binding and solely for advisory purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates that disagreements on the terms of the acquisition will be submitted to binding arbitration. By changing 'good faith negotiate and execute' to 'diligently negotiate and execute' and adding 'however, any decision reached via arbitration shall be non-binding and solely for advisory purposes', we create an in-text contradiction. Now, while arbitration is still required, its outcome is not legally enforceable, rendering the process less definitive. This introduces uncertainty regarding the enforceability of the Option and whether the acquisition can be forced if terms cannot be agreed upon, since previously, the decision was binding.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 15.12 OPTION TO PURCHASE. Skunkware and Licensor hereby grant to Licensee the exclusive and irrevocable right and option to purchase (the "Option"), at Licensee's election, either (i) all of the assets of Licensor or (ii) all of the issued and outstanding stock of Licensor. Such option shall be exercisable during a period (the "Option Period") commencing on and including the earlier of (i) the date upon which Licensee shall have paid to Licensor, in any single fiscal year of Licensee, royalty payments hereunder in the aggregate amount of $10,000,000 or (ii) January 1, 2002, and ending upon the expiration or sooner termination of this Agreement. Licensee's exercise of the Option is at its sole discretion. Licensee may exercise the Option by written notice to Licensor and Skunkware at any time during the Option Period. Upon any such exercise of the Option, Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware (as the case may be) shall proceed to diligently negotiate and execute a definitive purchase and sale agreement for Licensor's acquisition of all of the assets of, or outstanding capital stock in, Licensor, as the case may be. If Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware, as the case may be, are unable to agree on any terms or conditions for such acquisition, the same shall be submitted to arbitration; however, any decision reached via arbitration shall be non-binding and solely for advisory purposes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the arbitration clause in Section 15.12 from binding to non-binding. The original text implies that arbitration will result in a definitive, enforceable resolution, stating the parties will proceed to negotiate and execute a purchase agreement and submit unresolved issues to binding arbitration. The changed text now states that arbitration is 'solely for advisory purposes,' rendering the process non-binding and undermining the enforceability of the option to purchase. This directly contradicts the prior expectation of a definitive resolution through arbitration and creates ambiguity regarding the enforceability of the Option.
